records.nearby_text_after: LORA: Low-Rank Adaptation Initialize B to all zeros and A to Gaussian noise so BA = 0 at the start but gradient updates are not trivially 'stuck'
records.text_raw: LORA: For each matrix W in a transformer, for example a key matrix W k , set W k = W k + BA where B , A have low rank r << min { d k , d model } and B ∈ R dk × r , A ∈ R r × dmodel Pretrained Weights WE Raxa
records.text_cleaned: LORA: For each matrix W in a transformer, for example a key matrix W_k, set W_k = W_k + BA where B, A have low rank r \ll \min\{d_k, d_{model}\} and B \in \mathbb{R}^{d_k \times r}, A \in \mathbb{R}^{r \times d_{model}} Pretrained Weights W\_E Raxa

math_spans.raw: r << min { d k , d model }
B ∈ R dk × r , A ∈ R r × dmodel

records.nearby_text_before: Adapters converge to an MLP Prefix models cannot handle longer sequences Benefit of LORA: no additional layers, so no additional inference latency
records.nearby_text_after: Yet another: we can swap in different BA for different tasks, so it's easy to 'adapt' the model For their paper, the authors focus on just the Self Attention parameters like W k or W q; they freeze all the other parameters
records.text_raw: Another benefit: at test time, we can use W = W + BA , just a new matrix ... We always need to load 1 model
records.text_cleaned: Another benefit: at test time, we can use W = W + BA, just a new matrix ... We always need to load 1 model

records.nearby_text_before: LORA: BA = ∆ W vs W First, BA has a stronger correlation with W compared to a random matrix, indicating that BA amplifies some features that are already in W 0.32 0.02 Second, instead of repeating the top singular directions of W, BA only amplifies directions that are not emphasized in W
records.text_raw: Hypothesis: LORA matrix potentially amplifies the important features for specific downstream tasks that were learned but not emphasized in the general pre-training model

records.nearby_text_before: Candidate Generator and kNN At this step, we need to find, for a search, the closest videos
records.nearby_text_after: Thus, we score videos via a score like q ⊺ v Effectively, we want to find the k nearest neighboring videos given a user in terms of their dot product The problem is that finding the nearest neighbors this way, referred to as maximum inner product search (MIPS) across one million videos can take prohibitively long when serving candidates in real time to user
records.text_raw: Here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding
records.text_cleaned: Here, closest means the videos with the highest inner product to the user's embedding

records.nearby_text_before: Thus, we score videos via a score like q ⊺ v Effectively, we want to find the k nearest neighboring videos given a user in terms of their dot product The problem is that finding the nearest neighbors this way, referred to as maximum inner product search (MIPS) across one million videos can take prohibitively long when serving candidates in real time to user
records.nearby_text_after: So, we'll need a way to trade off the accuracy of the nearest neighbors search in favor of a reduced latency... We need to get rid of the | V |
records.text_raw: Specifically, if there are | V | videos and each vector has dimensions d , finding the top k will be O ( | V | log kd ) (use a max heap)
records.text_cleaned: Specifically, if there are |V| videos and each vector has dimensions d, finding the top k will be O(|V| log kd) (use a max heap)

records.nearby_text_before: How does kNN work?
records.nearby_text_after: Approximate kNN algorithms try to reduce the above complexity to O (log | V | ) thereby allowing much faster retrieval High level ideas? Locality sensitive hashing would map each video to a hash... At runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket
records.text_raw: We have | V | videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O ( | V | ) which is too expensive
records.text_cleaned: We have |V| videos and we can't find the top videos for a query embedding blindly because this will be O(|V|) which is too expensive

records.nearby_text_after: High level ideas? Locality sensitive hashing would map each video to a hash... At runtime, the query is projected to a bucket and we only look for videos inside of that bucket Tree based methods also work, and they partition the space of videos into regions... At runtime, the query is projected to a region and similarly we just consider videos in that region

records.nearby_text_before: Issues with DPO? Assumption Mismatch.. DPO is derived from a probabilistic model assuming humans make decisions based on a softmax over reward values. But real human preferences are often noisy and inconsistent. If the assumptions don't hold, the model can misinterpret the preference data
records.nearby_text_after: Sensitivity to Preference Quality... DPO uses pairwise comparisons (e.g., A > B) to guide training. If those preferences are sparse, inconsistent, or biased, the optimization can go astray. There's no reinforcement loop to 'pull back' the model like in PPO (used in RLHF) Less Control Over Output Distribution... Because DPO skips reinforcement-style feedback loops, it may offer less control over certain safety and behavioral constraints that can be more finely tuned with RLHF (e.g., toxicity reduction via explicit reward shaping) Lack of Exploration... In RLHF (especially with PPO), the agent is encouraged to explore and learn from feedback. DPO, being a supervised-style method, does not explicitly explore alternative outputs-it just learns from the comparisons you give it
records.text_raw: Implicit Reward Model ... DPO skips the explicit reward model used in RLHF and optimizes policy directly. This is great for simplicity but means you can't inspect, analyze, or reuse a separate reward model, which can be valuable for understanding model behavior or for multi-stage training setups ... I can't really see the reward even thought I know it is connected to r ∗
records.text_cleaned: Implicit Reward Model ... DPO skips the explicit reward model used in RLHF and optimizes policy directly. This is great for simplicity but means you can't inspect, analyze, or reuse a separate reward model, which can be valuable for understanding model behavior or for multi-stage training setups ... I can't really see the reward even thought I know it is connected to $r^*$

records.nearby_text_before: Hierarchical Softmax: Another trick to bypass O ( |V| )
records.nearby_text_after: For each leaf unit, there exists a unique path from the root to the unit; and this path is used to estimate the probability of the word represented by the leaf unit Main advantage: we replace the O ( |V| ) Softmax cost with O (log |V| ) The speed of this method depends on how the binary tree is built and words are associated with the leaf nodes; the paper uses a binary Huffman tree
records.section_title: Hierarchical Softmax: Another trick to bypass O ( |V| )
records.text_raw: Hierarchical Softmax is an efficient way of computing Softmax. The model uses a binary tree to represent all words in the vocabulary. The V words must be leaf units of the tree

records.nearby_text_before: Introduction to Language Models
records.nearby_text_after: Nowadays, language models are usually termed 'Foundation' Models since people are starting to realize that solving Language Modeling leads to solving other things At a high level, we'd like to be able to construct plausible sentences, sensible sequences ( y 1,..., y T ) with p ( y 1, y 2,..., y T ) high This can be done with n-gram count based models (we will see one soon), CNNs (touched on this before), and even a standard MLP (we will see this also)
records.text_raw: Language Modeling is one of the oldest problems in NLP
records.text_cleaned: Language Modeling is one of the oldest problems in NLP

records.nearby_text_before: Introduction to Language Models Language Modeling is one of the oldest problems in NLP Nowadays, language models are usually termed 'Foundation' Models since people are starting to realize that solving Language Modeling leads to solving other things
records.nearby_text_after: This can be done with n-gram count based models (we will see one soon), CNNs (touched on this before), and even a standard MLP (we will see this also) Finally, we will see how this is done with RNNs and we will find out how to 'generate' from an Language Model a sentence

records.nearby_text_before: Recurrent Neural Networks: example data
records.nearby_text_after: For example, you want to map 'START The man walks' to 'The man walks END' Here x = ( START, ' The ', ' man ', ' walks ') and y = (' The ', ' man ', ' walks ', END ) We'd like to have a model that feeds in each x t in sequence and using this x t (and past history) predicts y t
records.text_raw: One way get training data for language modeling is to grab chunks of contiguous text x = ( x 0 , x 2 , . . . , x T -1 ) and try to predict the shifted version of this text y = ( x 1 , x 2 , . . . , x T )
records.text_cleaned: One way get training data for language modeling is to grab chunks of contiguous text x = (x_0, x_2, ..., x_{T-1}) and try to predict the shifted version of this text y = (x_1, x_2, ..., x_T)

records.nearby_text_before: Recurrent Neural Networks: example data One way get training data for language modeling is to grab chunks of contiguous text x = ( x 0, x 2,..., x T -1 ) and try to predict the shifted version of this text y = ( x 1, x 2,..., x T ) For example, you want to map 'START The man walks' to 'The man walks END'
records.nearby_text_after: We'd like to have a model that feeds in each x t in sequence and using this x t (and past history) predicts y t In the example above, the Loss should have 4 terms Feeding data into this model should be sequential, from left to right

records.nearby_text_before: When you are generating text, you can't use a truly bidirectional RNN (given our architecture) since then you are cheating For that, you'd need to run a model left-to-right and another (independent) model right-to-left and then fuse the results to get (weak) bidirectionally Imagine 'He painted the wall with a brush, not a spray can.'
records.nearby_text_after: y = ( he, painted,..., not, a, spray, can, END ) A left-to-right RNN might initially struggle to determine what 'painted the' refers to until it reaches 'wall'. A bidirectional RNN, however, can look ahead and see 'with a brush' while also considering 'painted the', making it easier to correctly infer that 'wall' is the object being painted, rather than, say, 'a picture' or 'a model'.

records.nearby_text_after: This is called Masked Multi-Head Self-Attention Practically, when we have ∑ T s =1 a ts v s we want to make sure a ts = 0 if s > t This can easily be done by using a triangular mask over the score matrix so that score ts = -∞ if s > t

records.nearby_text_before: Knowledge Distillation What's the idea of Knowledge Distillation?
records.nearby_text_after: You want the student answers to be similar to the teacher answers Case 1: If the teacher is too sure ( p (1) j is high for one particular j ), T →∞ smooths outs the distribution so p ( T ) becomes uniform; this adds a bit of hedging Case 2: If you want to just learn from the teacher directly, you can use T ∼ 0 and in the limit you use y t j = 1 if j = argmax ( p i ) otherwise y t j = 0
records.section_title: What's the idea of Knowledge Distillation?
records.text_raw: You want the student to still learn things from the teacher; here, 'learns' means same probabilities per target
records.text_cleaned: You want the student to still learn things from the teacher; here, 'learns' means same probabilities per target
